hook: A 5,000-year-old underground necropolis carved three storeys into living rock
facts: Discovered by accident in 1902 by workers cutting cisterns for new houses
Three superimposed levels hewn from globigerina limestone over a 500 sqm area
Remains of an estimated 7,000 people were found inside
Red-ochre spirals on the ceilings are Malta's only prehistoric wall paintings
legend: Visitors swear the carved Oracle Room amplifies a low male voice through the whole complex; some claim the resonance alters mood and thought.
w: Workmen cutting cisterns for a new housing block in 1902 broke through into the dark and found three storeys of chambers gouged from the rock around 3000 BC, packed with the bones of some 7,000 dead. Spirals in red ochre still glow on the ceiling. They say the Oracle Room hums any low voice through the whole hill.

hook: The secret underground HQ where the Sicily invasion was directed, 150 feet down
facts: Tunnels expanded from Hospitaller-era slave quarters under the Saluting Battery, completed 1943
Eisenhower, Montgomery and Cunningham ran Operation Husky from here in 1943
Around 1,000 people worked the rooms, built to survive 500lb bomb hits
Taken over by NATO in 1967 as a Cold War submarine-tracking centre until 1977
w: Cut 150 feet under the Saluting Battery, this warren began as Hospitaller slave quarters before the British turned it into a bombproof war headquarters. From these rooms Eisenhower and Montgomery directed the 1943 invasion of Sicily. Later NATO used it to hunt Soviet submarines. A map table at the bottom of the rock.

hook: One of Europe's oldest working theatres, built in ten months in 1731
facts: Commissioned and self-funded by Grand Master Antonio Manoel de Vilhena in 1731
Built in only ten months, with a gilded Rococo auditorium of wood
Reputed Europe's third-oldest working theatre, oldest in the Commonwealth
Fell into use as a doss house in the 1860s before its revival
w: A grand master built this gilded little jewel box in 1731 'for the honest recreation of the people', and it went up in ten months. Three tiers of carved boxes curve around a wooden auditorium still in use today. When the new opera house opened it sank to a doss house, renting stalls for pennies a night. It outlived its rival.

hook: A bastion terrace where a noon gun has timed the harbour since 1824
facts: Built in the 1560s on or near an Ottoman battery site from the Great Siege
From 1824 a time-ball and noon gun let ships set their chronometers
Sits on the lower tier of St Peter and Paul Bastion below Upper Barrakka
Eight working 32-pounder replicas now fire signals at noon and 4pm
w: On a ledge below the Upper Barrakka crowds, gunners load a long row of cannon and fire one at the stroke of noon, just as they have since 1824. Captains down in the Grand Harbour once set their chronometers by the bang and the dropping time-ball. Cover your ears; the old siege bastion still speaks loudest at midday.

hook: A lived-in 16th-century palazzo with WWII bomb shelters cut into its own quarry
facts: Built in 1580 for Admiral Don Pietro la Rocca, a Knight of Malta
Still home to the de Piro family, who open over 50 rooms to visitors
Two old wells were cut into bomb shelters in the heavily bombed war years
The largest shelter served as a chapel for over 100 sheltering people
w: Behind a plain door on a busy street, the de Piro family still live among four centuries of clutter, portraits and a talking parrot. Below the state rooms, stairs drop into the rock to wartime shelters carved from the same quarry that built the house in 1580. Over a hundred neighbours once prayed down there through the raids.

hook: The sealed vault under St John's holding the Order's first eleven grand masters
facts: Cut beneath the high altar during the church's late-16th-century construction
Holds the first eleven grand masters who led the Order between 1522 and 1623
Includes Jean de Valette, hero of the 1565 siege and founder of the city
Decorated with a fresco cycle and carved sarcophagi, restored from 2007
w: Below the cathedral floor where coachloads admire Caravaggio, a barred chamber holds the men who actually built the place. Eleven grand masters lie here, among them Jean de Valette, who beat the Ottomans in 1565 and laid out the city that bears his name. A bronze effigy, hands crossed, in the gloom beneath the altar.

hook: One of the last surviving inquisition palaces, complete with cells and tribunal
facts: Began as the Castellania courthouse in the early 16th century
Seat of the Maltese Inquisition from 1574 to 1798
Two of its inquisitors later became popes, Alexander VII and Innocent XII
Holds tribunal chambers, prison cells and the inquisitors' apartments
w: Down a Birgu side street hides one of the very few inquisition palaces left standing anywhere. For two centuries clerics tried heretics in its tribunal hall and locked them in cells whose walls still carry scratched graffiti. Two inquisitors who worked here went on to wear the papal tiara. Power, faith and a prison under one roof.

hook: An 18th-century barracks atop a rock-cut shelter that hid hundreds from the Blitz
facts: Housed in an 18th-century barracks used as an air-raid precautions centre
Sits over a vast rock-cut shelter with dormitories, a chapel and a birth room
Helmets are issued for the low passages of the restored shelter
Birgu sat in Dockyard Creek, one of the most bombed places of the war
w: An old barracks at the gate of Birgu sits on a secret it shared with hundreds of terrified families. Down narrow rock stairs runs a hand-dug maze of shelter cubicles, a chapel, even a room where babies were born underground during the raids. They hand you a helmet for the low ceilings. This creek was bombed almost to dust.

hook: A Victorian battery built around a single monstrous 100-ton Armstrong gun
facts: Built 1878-1886 above the shore east of the Grand Harbour entrance
Armed with an Armstrong 100-ton rifled muzzle-loader firing one-ton shells
Only one other such gun survives, at Gibraltar's Napier of Magdala Battery
Now staffed by costumed re-enactors who fire drills and muskets
w: The Victorians grew so alarmed by Italian ironclads that they sank a whole fort around one absurd gun: an Armstrong 100-tonner that hurled a one-ton shell three miles to punch through warship armour. Only one twin survives, at Gibraltar. Re-enactors now work the loading gear by hand and fire muskets in the dust.

hook: The old Royal Naval Bakery, modelled on Windsor Castle, that fed the fleet
facts: Built 1842-1845 by William Scamp on the Order's former arsenal site
Its facade was reportedly inspired by Windsor Castle
Twelve ovens turned out nearly 14,000 kg of bread and biscuit a day
Closed in the 1950s and reopened as the maritime museum in 1992
w: This crenellated pile on the Birgu waterfront looks like a stray bit of Windsor Castle, which the architect more or less intended. Behind the battlements sat twelve ovens baking 14,000 kilos of bread and ship's biscuit a day for the Mediterranean Fleet. The fleet steamed off, the ovens went cold, and the models moved in.

hook: A bombed-out 1860s opera house left as roofless ruins beside the city gate
facts: Designed by Edward Middleton Barry and built 1862-1866
Gutted by an accidental fire in 1873, then rebuilt
Devastated by Luftwaffe bombers on 7 April 1942
Renzo Piano framed the surviving columns as an open-air theatre in 2013
w: Just inside the gate stand the broken columns of the grand opera house that English architect Barry raised in 1866. Fire gutted it once; in April 1942 the Luftwaffe finished the job, leaving a heap of stone the country argued over for seventy years. Renzo Piano finally roofed it with sky and put a stage among the ruins.

hook: The terminal tower of a 1615 aqueduct that finally brought Valletta its water
facts: End of the Wignacourt Aqueduct, inaugurated 21 April 1615
Aqueduct carried spring water from Dingli and Rabat to the new capital
Built by 600 workers under Bolognese engineer Bontadino de Bontadini
Round structure with a fountain, horse trough and a carved fleur-de-lis on top
w: For decades the new capital drank from cisterns and worried. Then in 1615 water arrived along miles of arches from the hills of Rabat, paid for largely by Grand Master Wignacourt, and this round little tower by the Argotti gardens marked the end of the line. A fountain, a horse trough, a fleur-de-lis crown. The drought, broken.

hook: The Order's last great building, holding the archives of the Knights of Malta
facts: Erected 1786-1796 to Stefano Ittar's design, the Order's final major work
Stood empty for years after Napoleon expelled the Knights in 1798
Holds the archives of the Order of St John, transferred here in 1937
Houses the largest collection of Melitensia alongside the university's
w: The Knights finished their last great building in 1796 and barely got to use it before Napoleon threw them off the island. So this hushed colonnaded library stood empty for years, then filled with the Order's own archives, centuries of bulls, ledgers and crusader paperwork. Readers still murmur under the arches above the square.

hook: A stone watch turret carved with an eye and an ear at the tip of Senglea
facts: Built in 1551 at the seaward point of the Senglea peninsula
Carved with an eye, an ear and a crane symbolising vigilance
Gave early warning of Ottoman attack on the Grand Harbour
Now an emblematic landmark of the Three Cities
legend: The carved eye and ear are said to mean the sentry never slept, forever watching and listening for the enemy galleys.
w: At the very tip of Senglea a little stone turret leans over the water, carved with a staring eye and a cupped ear. Cut in 1551, it warned of Ottoman galleys creeping into the Grand Harbour. The legend goes that the symbols meant the watchman never slept, always watching, always listening. The whole harbour spread out beneath it.

hook: The narrow lane the sailors called The Gut, once all bars, jazz and brothels
facts: Long a duelling spot for the Knights, then a courtesans' quarter
Became the city's red-light strip of music halls and bars in the 19th-20th c.
British and American servicemen nicknamed it The Gut
Emptied after independence in 1964, now revived as a nightlife lane
legend: Knights forbidden to brawl elsewhere are said to have slipped into this narrow alley to settle quarrels with the sword, where the walls were too close to flee.
w: Knights forbidden to duel slipped into this slot of a street, too narrow to run, to cross swords. Later it became The Gut, where sailors poured ashore for jazz, gin and ladies of ill repute behind a hundred bar signs. Independence emptied it overnight. The neon faded; the stories did not.

hook: The harbour fortress that was the Order's headquarters through the 1565 siege
facts: Stands on the site of the medieval Castrum Maris, older still beneath
Headquarters of the Order during the Great Siege of 1565
Reinforced over centuries by Ferramolino and de Grunenberg
Commissioned by the Royal Navy as a stone frigate, HMS Egmont then St Angelo
legend: A windowless cell called the guva is remembered as a pit where disgraced knights were lowered and left; some say a haunting lingers in the fort's oldest corners.
w: This blunt fist of stone at the harbour's heart has been fortified since a temple to Astarte supposedly stood here. From its walls the Order held out through the murderous summer of 1565, and the Royal Navy later ran it as a ship that never sailed. They say a knight's prison pit, the guva, still holds its chill in the rock.

hook: The Knights' holy infirmary with a ward 155 metres long, where patients ate off silver
facts: Begun in 1574 under Grand Master Jean de la Cassiere
Great Ward ran over 155 metres, among the longest in the world
Held up to 2,500 patients and ranked among Europe's leading hospitals
Now the Mediterranean Conference Centre after use until 1920
legend: The Knights are said to have served even the poorest patients on plates of silver, partly as charity and partly to deter theft of the costly tableware.
w: The Hospitaller Knights took their nursing seriously enough to build a ward over 155 metres long, one of the grandest in Europe, where they fed the sick from silver plates. Up to 2,500 lay here above the harbour. It served until 1920, then bombs, then conferences. Faded grandeur measured by the length of a corridor.

hook: The star fort that bled the Ottomans in 1565 and now guards the Gladiator Faith
facts: Built by the Knights in 1552 in four months at the peninsula's tip
Fell after a month-long siege in 1565 that cost the Ottomans dearly
Now holds the National War Museum across seven galleries
Displays the Gloster Sea Gladiator Faith and Malta's George Cross
w: Thrown up in four months in 1552, this star fort held the harbour mouth, and in 1565 its small garrison fought to the last man, costing the Ottomans weeks and thousands before it fell. Inside now sits the war museum: the battered Gladiator biplane Faith, and the George Cross a whole island was awarded for not giving in.

hook: A quiet bastion-top garden with a neoclassical tomb watching over Marsamxett
facts: Laid out atop St John's and St Michael's Bastions on the western walls
Named for Francis, Marquis of Hastings, governor 1824-1826
His neoclassical mausoleum stands under a canopy in the garden
Looks out over Marsamxett Harbour, Manoel Island and Floriana
w: Most visitors crowd the Barrakka on the grand-harbour side and never find this scruffy walk along the western ramparts. Up here a governor of Malta, the Marquis of Hastings, lies under a sober neoclassical canopy he supposedly chose himself, gazing across Marsamxett to Manoel Island. Bastion grass, a tomb, and the quiet side of town.

hook: An 1837 cafe whose Cospicua original was bombed flat in the war
facts: Founded in 1837 by the Cordina family in Cospicua, across the harbour
A German bomb destroyed the original premises, prompting the 1944 move to Valletta
Ranks among the oldest cafes in Europe, on pedestrianised Republic Street
Frescoed ceilings and a marble standing bar in the old confectionery style
w: The Cordina family started selling sweets in Cospicua back in 1837, until a German bomb flattened the shop and chased them across the harbour to Valletta in 1944. Now among Europe's older cafes, it pours coffee under a frescoed ceiling on the city's grand street. Order a kannol at the marble bar and watch the whole town drift past.
